lessons.title: Open Raising From Late Position
lessons.content: Late position gives you more information, so you can enter the pot with a wider range and stronger pressure on the blinds.

lessons.title: Defending the Blinds
lessons.content: When facing a steal, defend with hands that keep playability and avoid overcalling dominated spots.

questions.title: Button open raise
questions.scenario: You are on the button in a soft cash game. The players in the blinds are passive.
questions.explanation: The button should open wider because you act last postflop and steal more uncontested pots.

choices.label: Raise a wider range
choices.explanation: This uses position to win pots before the flop and keeps pressure on the blinds.

choices.label: Limp most hands
choices.explanation: Limping gives up initiative and misses profitable steals.

choices.label: Fold everything except premiums
choices.explanation: That range is far too tight for the button.

questions.title: Small blind defense
questions.scenario: The button opens and the action folds to you in the small blind.
questions.explanation: Defend selectively. Keep hands that can realize equity well and avoid calling with weak dominated hands.

choices.label: Call with a balanced defend range
choices.explanation: A disciplined defend keeps enough playability without overdefending.

choices.label: Call every suited hand
choices.explanation: Too many marginal hands make postflop decisions harder and leak value.

choices.label: Fold all but aces and kings
choices.explanation: That is far too tight against a button steal.

lessons.title: Turning Ratios Into Decisions
lessons.content: Pot odds matter because they convert chip prices into clear continue or fold thresholds. Train yourself to compare the cost of a call with the total pot before guessing.

lessons.title: Equity Shortcuts Under Pressure
lessons.content: You do not need exact combinatorics in every hand. Build a few shortcut benchmarks so draws, pairs, and overcards map quickly to realistic equity ranges.

questions.title: Flush draw facing a turn bet
questions.scenario: You hold a flush draw on the turn and face a half-pot bet.
questions.explanation: A half-pot bet offers a 3:1 price. If your draw plus implied value clears that threshold, calling stays disciplined.

choices.label: Compare the call price with your drawing equity
choices.explanation: Pot odds are a pricing problem first, not a feeling-based decision.

choices.label: Fold every draw because the turn is expensive
choices.explanation: Some draws remain profitable even on later streets.

choices.label: Jam immediately with all draws
choices.explanation: Aggression depends on fold equity and stack depth, not just the presence of a draw.

questions.title: Gutshot versus a small river price
questions.scenario: You are chasing a gutshot and face a very small price relative to the pot.
questions.explanation: Small bets widen your continuing range because the required equity threshold drops. The correct response comes from the price, not the label on your draw.

choices.label: Use the pot price to decide if the draw can continue
choices.explanation: Cheap prices can justify continuing even with weaker draws.

choices.label: Fold because gutshots are always too weak
choices.explanation: That ignores the effect of price on profitability.

choices.label: Call only if you also have top pair
choices.explanation: Pair strength matters, but the core concept here is pot pricing.
